lesson_title: Array indexing and length using enumerators

cards.front: Why use enumerators for array indices instead of magic numbers?
cards.back: Enumerators provide meaningful names, improve code readability, prevent errors, and make it easier to maintain and modify code when adding/removing elements.
cards.source: 16.9 - Array indexing and length using enumerators

cards.text: To use enumerators as array indices, define an {{c1::enum}} or {{c1::enum class}} with values for each index position.
cards.source: 16.9 - Array indexing and length using enumerators

cards.question: How would you define an enum for array indices?
cards.code: std::vector<int> scores; // test scores for: math, english, science
cards.answer:
enum class Subject { math, english, science, max_subjects };
std::vector<int> scores(static_cast<int>(Subject::max_subjects));
cards.explanation: Define enum with named indices and a max value to determine array size.
cards.source: 16.9 - Array indexing and length using enumerators

cards.concept: Using a max_value enumerator to determine array length
cards.explanation:
Add a max/count enumerator as the last value in your enum:
  • Automatically represents the number of elements
  • Updates automatically when you add/remove enumerators
  • Prevents off-by-one errors
  • Makes code self-documenting
examples: enum class Color { red, green, blue, max_colors };
cards.source: 16.9 - Array indexing and length using enumerators

cards.text: To get the array size from an enum, add a final enumerator like {{c1::max_count}} or {{c1::max_items}}, which will have a value equal to the {{c2::number of other enumerators}}.
cards.source: 16.9 - Array indexing and length using enumerators

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.code:
enum class Item { sword, shield, potion, max_items };
std::cout << static_cast<int>(Item::max_items);
cards.explanation: max_items is the 4th enumerator, with value 3 (after sword=0, shield=1, potion=2).
cards.source: 16.9 - Array indexing and length using enumerators

cards.front: Why do you need to cast enum class values when using them as array indices?
cards.back: enum class is strongly typed and doesn't implicitly convert to int. Use static_cast<int>(enum_value) to convert for array indexing.
cards.source: 16.9 - Array indexing and length using enumerators

cards.text: To access an array using an enum class: vec[{{c1::static_cast<int>(EnumClass::value)}}]
cards.source: 16.9 - Array indexing and length using enumerators

cards.question: Write code to access element using enum
cards.explanation: Cast the enum value to int to use as array index.
cards.source: 16.9 - Array indexing and length using enumerators

cards.front: What advantage does using enums for indices provide when refactoring?
cards.back: If you add/remove items from the enum, the max_value enumerator automatically updates, and all code using it adjusts automatically. No need to hunt for magic numbers throughout code.
cards.source: 16.9 - Array indexing and length using enumerators

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
enum class Color { red, green, blue };
std::vector<int> v(Color::blue);
cards.answer: Cannot implicitly convert enum class to int. Need: std::vector<int> v(static_cast<int>(Color::blue));
cards.explanation: enum class requires explicit casting. Also, blue=2, so this creates a vector of size 2, not 3. Should use max_colors.
cards.source: 16.9 - Array indexing and length using enumerators
